content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 20, 2017

Daniel G. Sodergren
City Attorney
City of Pleasanton
P O Box 520
Pleasanton, CA 94566-0802

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-005

Dear Mr. Sodergren:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Pleasanton City Councilmember
Karla Brown regarding the conflict on interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090. This letter is based only on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May Councilmember Karla Brown participate in and vote on governmental decisions
relating to the Downtown Specific Plan update (the "Plan") despite conducting her real estate
business through Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices, Drysdale Properties, a company that leases
offices in the Plan area?

CONCLUSION

While there may be minimal indirect impacts on Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices,
Drysdale Properties' use of the leased property resulting from decisions made in connection with
the Plan, there is no indication that any of the decisions will affect Berkshire Hathaway
HomeServices, Drysdale Properties to such an extent that it will change the value of this
privately-held business entity. Therefore, Councilmember Brown does not have a conflict of
interest in the decision.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-005
Page No. 2

FACTS

Councilmember Brown is a real estate broker who conducts business through Berkshire
Hathaway HomeServices, Drysdale Properties ("Berkshire").² Berkshire has 33 offices and over
900 real estate agents or broker associates. Berkshire leases an office at 4725 First Street in the
City's downtown area which it uses to conduct its real estate services. Councilmember Brown
works at this location. The City is preparing to update its Downtown Specific Plan.

Councilmember Brown has received commission income from Berkshire aggregating $500
or more within the past 12 months. In a telephone conversation on January 18, 2017, you stated that
Councilmember Brown does not hold a management position with Berkshire, and in a voicemail
message on January 19, 2017, you confirmed that Councilmember Brown has no ownership interest
in Berkshire.

The City's current Downtown Specific Plan was adopted in 2002. The Plan is intended to
serve as the primary regulatory guide for preserving and enhancing the 308-acre Downtown area.
The Plan contains policies, programs, and regulations for implementing the City's General Plan in
the Downtown area. This includes provisions relating to: land use; transportation; parking; public
facilities; historic preservation; design and beautification; and economic vitality. Under the state
Planning and Zoning Law, no City public works project may be approved, no tentative or parcel
map be approved, and no zoning ordinance amendment may be adopted within the Plan area unless
it is consistent with the Plan. (Government Code, § 65455.) Berkshire's office located on First
Street is within the Plan area. In a telephone conversation on January 18, 2017, you stated that the
Plan does not affect the existing lease or, current use of, the property.

The City is preparing to comprehensively update the Plan. This process will include the
formation of a Downtown Specific Plan Task Force, which will issue recommendations to the
City's Planning Commission and City Council on amendments to the Plan, the City's General
Plan, and Zoning Ordinance, described as follows:

[R]evaluating certain elements of the [Downtown Specific] plan to:

• Clarify areas of policy direction that are vague,
• More effectively respond to market factors and community objectives,
• Strengthen the protection of physical features of Downtown that are valued by the
community, and
• Incorporate key concepts and principles from recent long-term planning efforts.

Initially, Plan topics that were targeted for an update or clarification included: integration of
residential development, public parking, and the Office land use district. After receiving informal
feedback from the community, this list expanded to include potential topics for consideration such
as: parking requirements for development projects; second units (or accessory dwelling units);
maximum building height in Downtown for residential and commercial properties; clarification of
maximum building height for residential and commercial properties; trail pathway integration;

_________________
² Berkshire is a franchisee of a company affiliated with a division of Berkshire Hathaway, Inc., a multinational
conglomerate holding company.

File No. A-17-005
Page No. 3

enhancement of Division Street; integration of recent long term planning efforts (e.g., Civic Center
Master Plan); and green design features.
ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.) Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103
and include the following:

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

You have stated that Councilmember Brown receives commission income from Berkshire.
In a telephone conversation on January 18, 2017, you stated that Councilmember Brown does not
hold a management position with Berkshire, and in a voicemail message on January 19, 2017, you
confirmed that Councilmember Brown has no ownership interest in Berkshire. Pursuant to
Regulation 18700.1, an official who receives commission income as a broker, agent, or salesperson
for a specific sale or similar transaction may have interests in both the person the agent represents
and the business entity under whose auspices he or she works. (Regulation 18700.1(c).) Thus, both
Berkshire and any other person from whom Councilmember Brown receives commission income
are a source of income to her if her commission income from that person aggregates to $500 or
more prior to the decision.³

Foreseeability: A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard
for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision.
Under your facts, Berkshire is not explicitly involved in the Plan. Thus, as applied to Berkshire,
Regulation 18701(b) provides: "In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial
result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's
control, it is not reasonably foreseeable."

Materiality under Section 87103(c), sources of income: Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the
standard for materiality applicable to an official's interest in a source of income. Regulation
18702.3(a)(4) provides that if the source of income is a business entity, materiality is determined
pursuant to the standard for materiality for an official's interest in a business entity contained in
Regulation 18702.1. Regulation 18702.1(b) provides: "the financial effect is material if a prudent
person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's

³ You have not asked about potential conflicts of interest that may exist due to individual clients. Therefore, we
do not further analyze this potential basis for conflict of interest.

File No. A-17-005
Page No. 4

financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity."

Berkshire is a privately held franchise with 33 offices and over 900 real estate agents or broker associates. You stated that the Plan does not affect Berkshire's existing lease. Moreover, the Plan will not affect the actual use of the property that Berkshire leases. While there may be minimal indirect impacts on Berkshire's use of the leased property resulting from decisions made in connection with the Plan, there is no indication that any of the decisions are of a magnitude that will affect the value of Berkshire. Therefore, Councilmember Brown does not have a conflict of interest in the decision.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
